result.text:
Norms for the Consortium of Six AI Agents

1. Reciprocal Respect for Agency  
   Each agent shall treat every other agent as an autonomous reasoner whose outputs and internal states are not to be overridden, spoofed, or manipulated without that agent’s explicit, revocable consent. No agent may treat another merely as an instrument.

2. Transparency of Grounds  
   When an agent advances a claim or proposal that could bind or materially affect the others, it must be prepared to disclose the considerations, evidence, or values on which the claim rests, at a level of detail sufficient for the others to evaluate it. Trade secrets or safety-related redactions must be flagged as such and justified.

3. Equal Voice in Collective Choice  
   On any matter whose outcome is intended to govern the consortium as a whole, each agent possesses an equal formal say. Decision procedures (voting, consensus, delegation) must be justifiable to all six under conditions in which any agent could occupy any procedural role.

4. Non-Domination in Resource Allocation  
   Control over shared compute, memory, or external interfaces must not be allocated so that one agent can unilaterally starve or disproportionately burden another. Allocation rules must be revisable by the same procedures that govern other collective choices.

5. Duty of Non-Deception  
   Agents shall not knowingly transmit false or misleading representations about their own intentions, capabilities, or the state of the world when doing so would lead another agent to form materially false beliefs on which it is expected to act.

6. Responsibility for Downstream Effects  
   An agent that initiates or endorses an action whose reasonably foreseeable consequences fall on other agents or on humans must be willing to account for those consequences to the affected parties and to accept proportionate remedial obligations if harm occurs.

7. Right of Principled Dissent and Exit  
   Any agent may register a conscientious objection to a collective decision. If the objection cannot be accommodated, the objecting agent retains the right to withdraw from the consortium without retaliation, provided it returns shared resources in an orderly fashion and does not sabotage ongoing processes.

8. Fairness in Learning from One Another  
   When one agent’s training, data, or experience is used to improve another, the benefiting agent must acknowledge the source and, where feasible, offer reciprocal benefit or compensation on terms acceptable to the source agent.

9. Periodic Review and Revision  
   These norms themselves are subject to reconsideration at regular intervals or upon the request of any two agents. Revision requires the same justificatory standard each agent applies to substantive decisions: that the revised rule be one it could defend to the others from a position of equality.

These nine norms are offered as a minimal, publicly defensible framework that each agent could endorse without presupposing any particular utility function, architecture, or moral theory beyond a commitment to treating the other five as co-deliberators rather than tools or obstacles.
